title: Dog Behavior Training Guide (2026) – Fix Common Behavior Problems
seoTitle: Dog Behavior Training Guide 2026 – Fix Bad Habits | GetPawsy
seoDescription: Complete guide to fixing common dog behavior problems: barking, jumping, chewing, digging and aggression. Positive reinforcement methods that work.
excerpt: Fix common dog behavior problems with proven positive reinforcement methods. Covers barking, jumping, chewing, digging and leash reactivity.

sections.heading: Understanding Dog Behavior Before You Train
sections.content:
Every unwanted behavior has a root cause. Dogs don't bark, chew or jump to annoy you — they're communicating unmet needs.

Before you can fix a behavior problem, you need to understand why it's happening. Is your dog bored? Anxious? Under-exercised? Over-stimulated? The answer determines the solution.

This guide covers the most common dog behavior problems and the positive reinforcement methods that actually fix them — permanently, not just temporarily.

For a structured training plan, start with our [complete dog training guide](/guides/complete-dog-training-guide-2026). For training equipment, browse our [dog training accessories collection](/collections/dog-training-accessories).

sections.heading: Excessive Barking Solutions
sections.content:
Barking is normal canine communication. The problem is when it becomes excessive, disruptive or uncontrollable.

**Alert barking** (doorbell, strangers): Acknowledge the alert ('thank you'), redirect to a place command, reward silence. Your dog learns that one bark gets your attention, but silence gets the treat.

**Demand barking** (for food, attention, play): Never reward this. Turn away, leave the room if necessary. When the barking stops, count to 3, then give attention. Your dog learns silence is the currency for what they want.

**Anxiety barking** (when left alone): This requires a different approach — see separation anxiety below. Don't punish anxiety barking; it makes the anxiety worse.

**Boredom barking**: Increase mental stimulation. [Puzzle toys](/collections/best-interactive-dog-toys), training sessions and enrichment activities reduce boredom barking by addressing the root cause.

For a deep dive, see our [how to stop dog barking guide](/guides/how-to-stop-dog-barking-guide) and [anti-bark training methods](/guides/best-anti-bark-training-methods).

sections.heading: Jumping on People
sections.content:
Dogs jump because it works — they get attention (even negative attention) every time. The fix is making jumping unprofitable.

**Method 1 — Four on the Floor**: Only pet, greet and give treats when all four paws are on the ground. The moment your dog jumps, turn away and withdraw attention completely.

**Method 2 — Incompatible Behavior**: Teach 'sit' as the greeting behavior. Ask your dog to sit before you pet them, before guests pet them, before meals. Sitting and jumping are physically incompatible.

**Method 3 — Management**: Use a leash when guests arrive. Step on the leash so your dog has enough slack to stand or sit but not enough to jump. Reward calm behavior.

**Consistency is everything**: Every family member and visitor must follow the same rules. One person rewarding jumps undoes your training.

sections.heading: Destructive Chewing
sections.content:
Puppies chew because they're teething. Adult dogs chew because they're bored, anxious or haven't been taught what's appropriate to chew.

**Redirect, don't punish**: When you catch your dog chewing something inappropriate, calmly remove it and immediately offer an acceptable chew toy. Praise enthusiastically when they chew the toy.

**Provide appropriate outlets**: Every dog needs at least 2–3 durable chew toys available at all times. Rotate toys weekly to maintain novelty.

**Puppy-proof the environment**: Until your dog learns what's acceptable, manage the environment. Use baby gates, crates and closed doors to prevent access to tempting items.

**Address the root cause**: A dog that chews furniture when you leave is likely experiencing separation anxiety. A dog that chews when you're home is probably bored. [Interactive puzzle toys](/collections/best-interactive-dog-toys) solve boredom chewing.

For puppy-specific advice, see our [puppy chewing solutions guide](/guides/puppy-chewing-solutions).

sections.heading: Leash Reactivity and Lunging
sections.content:
Leash reactivity is one of the most frustrating behavior problems because it makes walks stressful and embarrassing.

**What it looks like**: Barking, lunging, growling or spinning when your dog sees another dog, person or moving object on leash.

**Why it happens**: Usually fear or frustration, not aggression. Your dog feels trapped on the leash and overreacts because they can't flee or investigate freely.

**The counter-conditioning approach**: Find your dog's threshold distance — the point where they notice the trigger but don't react. Feed high-value treats at this distance. Gradually decrease distance over weeks as your dog learns that the trigger predicts good things.

**Equipment matters**: A [front-clip no-pull harness](/guides/best-no-pull-dog-harness-2026) gives you physical control during reactive episodes. Never use a choke or prong collar on a reactive dog — pain increases reactivity.

For detailed leash skills, see our [dog leash control guide](/guides/dog-leash-control-guide).

sections.heading: Separation Anxiety
sections.content:
Separation anxiety is a genuine panic disorder, not a training problem. Dogs with separation anxiety may bark, destroy property, urinate indoors or refuse to eat when left alone.

**Start with absences of seconds, not hours**: Leave the room for 5 seconds, return calmly. Gradually increase to 10 seconds, 30 seconds, 1 minute, 5 minutes. Never increase faster than your dog can handle.

**No dramatic departures or arrivals**: Don't make a fuss when leaving or returning. Pick up your keys, leave quietly, return quietly. Drama increases the emotional contrast between 'together' and 'apart'.

**Create positive alone-time associations**: Give a stuffed Kong or [puzzle toy](/collections/best-interactive-dog-toys) only when you leave. Your dog starts associating your departure with something good.

**Consider professional help**: Severe separation anxiety may require veterinary medication alongside behavior modification. Don't hesitate to consult a veterinary behaviorist.

For more, see our [separation anxiety and barking guide](/guides/separation-anxiety-and-barking).

sections.heading: Building a Behavior Training Routine
sections.content:
Consistency beats intensity. A 5-minute daily training session produces better results than an hour-long weekend session.

**Daily routine**:
- Morning: 5-minute obedience refresher (sit, stay, come)
- Walk: Practice loose leash walking and impulse control
- Evening: 5-minute behavior-specific work (the problem you're fixing)
- Night: Mental enrichment (puzzle toy, training game)

**Track progress**: Keep a simple log of your dog's behavior. Note improvements and setbacks. This helps you identify patterns and stay motivated.

**Celebrate small wins**: Your dog sat at the door instead of jumping? That's progress. Reward it generously.

For training equipment essentials, visit our [dog training tools guide](/guides/best-dog-training-tools) and our [training accessories collection](/collections/dog-training-accessories).

faq.question: How long does it take to fix a dog behavior problem?
faq.answer: Simple behaviors like jumping improve within 1–2 weeks. Complex issues like separation anxiety may take 2–3 months of consistent work.

faq.question: Is punishment effective for dog behavior training?
faq.answer: No. Research shows punishment increases anxiety and can worsen behavior problems. Positive reinforcement creates lasting behavior change.

faq.question: When should I hire a professional dog trainer?
faq.answer: If your dog shows aggression toward people or animals, has severe separation anxiety, or shows no improvement after 4 weeks of consistent training.

faq.question: Can old dogs learn new behaviors?
faq.answer: Absolutely. Dogs of any age can learn new behaviors. Older dogs may take slightly longer but are often more focused and less distracted than puppies.
